title: The Silent Partner
blurb: The queen of couture was always the hand behind the throne. Until the crown was stolen. Now she’s coming for everything—tonight, Fashion Week begins. And Adrian Voss doesn’t know he’s already lost.
comparable: For fans of *The Devil Wears Prada* by Lauren Weisberger, *Sharp Objects* by Gillian Flynn

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The Greene Street atelier smelled of lavender and cut paper. Sunlight slanted through the high windows, striping the worktable where Vivienne Moreau sliced navy gabardine with surgical precision. The scissors snipped once, twice—clean, definitive. A sleeve pattern fluttered to the floor. She didn’t bend to retrieve it.

Lila Chen tapped two knuckles on the open doorframe. “The jersey supplier is here early.”

Vivienne’s wrist didn’t slow. “Send them in.”

“You don’t want to take the meeting?”

“I don’t take meetings I didn’t schedule.”

Lila stepped aside. A woman in a charcoal coat stepped over the threshold, her heels silent against the hardwood. She carried a swatch book like a peace offering.

“Ms. Moreau.” The supplier’s voice was smooth, practiced. “We’ve been trying to reach you for weeks.”

Vivienne cut another line. “I know.”

The swatches thudded onto the table. Vivienne ignored them.

Lila hovered, fingers twisting the hem of her blazer. “She’s here about the Lyon order.”

“What order?”

Lila’s throat worked. “The one Adrian’s team requested. The one they said we couldn’t fulfill.”

Vivienne’s fingers tightened around the scissors. The supplier didn’t flinch.

“Adrian.” The name tasted stale. “He sent his own courier for our fabric last week. Didn’t even have the decency to ask.”

The supplier nodded. “He paid double. Insisted on exclusive access. Said Moreau Atelier was winding down.”

Vivienne set the scissors down. Her nails were short, unpainted. “Sit down, Lila.”

Lila obeyed.

The supplier exhaled, a controlled release. “I can’t justify cutting you off completely. Not without a formal notice.”

Vivienne reached for the swatch book. Her thumb brushed the edge of a midnight-blue sample. “I’ll sign whatever you need.”

Lila’s breath hitched.

The supplier slid a sheet of paper from her portfolio. “Just the standard dissolution form. One signature.”

Vivienne reached for a pen. She didn’t read the document.

Lila’s voice cracked. “Vivienne—”

The pen hovered.

The supplier’s phone buzzed. She glanced at the screen, then back at Vivienne. “I’ll give you a moment.”

She retreated to the far corner of the room, tapping at her phone. Lila leaned in, voice low.

“You can’t sign that.”

Vivienne signed.

Lila’s fingers dug into the table. “He’s dismantling us. He’s rewriting history before our eyes.”

Vivienne capped the pen. “History is written by those who hold the scissors.”

The supplier returned. “All set?”

Vivienne nodded.

The supplier tucked the signed form into her bag. “I’ll inform Adrian’s office you’re no longer a concern.”

The door clicked shut behind her.

Lila exhaled sharply. “We’re cut off. Again.”

Vivienne reached for a forgotten sketch tucked beneath the fabric remnants—an old Moreau Atelier design, ink smudged at the edges. She slid it across the table.

Lila stared. “What’s this?”

“Proof.”

“Of what?”

Vivienne stood, the chair scraping back. She crossed the room and opened a cabinet. Inside rested a stack of identical sketches, each one a ghost of a collection past. She pulled one free, the paper rigid with age.

Lila’s eyes flicked between the two. “I don’t understand.”

Vivienne returned to the worktable. She placed the fresh sketch atop the older one. The lines matched. The technique was unmistakable.

Lila’s voice dropped. “Adrian—”

“Is using my work.”

Silence filled the atelier. Outside, a delivery truck rumbled past on Greene Street. The sound was ordinary. Everything felt ordinary.

Lila’s phone buzzed. She pulled it from her pocket, frowning. “It’s Julian.”

Vivienne didn’t look up. “Take it.”

Lila’s thumb hovered over the screen. “He’s at the Mercer Street showroom. Says it’s urgent. Some press leak about ‘borrowed ideas’ in Adrian’s new collection.”

Vivienne cut a single length of thread, snipped it clean.

Lila swallowed. “He wants to know if you’re—”

Vivienne looked at her. Only for a second.

Lila ended the call.

Vivienne folded the sketch in half, then in half again. She tucked it into the breast pocket of her unstructured jacket.

Lila watched, still holding her phone. “I should warn him.”

“Warn who?”

“Julian. He’s Adrian’s lead pattern cutter. He’s got access to everything.”

Vivienne reached for the navy fabric. The scissors rose.

“Let him see.”

Chapter 2

The Mercer Street showroom smelled of bergamot and ambition. Tall windows stretched toward a sky the color of a freshly cut invoice. Buyers in sharp coats milled near the back, fingers wrapped around tablets, eyes locked on the empty center where the next Voss & Co collection would debut. Journalists stood shoulder to shoulder, murmuring into recorders, their black lenses blinking like startled eyes.

Adrian Voss stepped from behind a silk curtain, his pale blue shirt immaculate, his hair tousled with practiced effort. He cupped the microphone, testing its pop.

Nell Whitmore appeared beside him, a sleek black portfolio tucked under one arm. She wore a dress the color of gunmetal, the hem just low enough to flirt with the knee. Her smile was a blade polished to a mirror finish.

“Good morning,” Adrian said. The room stilled. “Thank you for being here when we need you most.”

He paused. The audience leaned in.

“We’re restructuring.”

A beat. Then, the rustle of fabric, the sharp inhale of a thousand collective lungs.

Nell stepped forward. She opened the portfolio. The click of the latch echoed like a gavel. “Effective immediately, Moreau Atelier is dissolved. Fabric shipments are redirected. Contracts are severed. The Moreau name will no longer appear on our production schedules.”

A woman in the third row dropped her notebook. Pages scattered like startled birds.

Adrian lifted a hand. “This isn’t a reflection on quality—”

“It’s a reflection on legacy,” Nell cut in. Her voice was steel wrapped in silk. “Vivienne Moreau’s work no longer aligns with our vision. She has—chosen to step away.”

A ripple ran through the crowd. Someone’s phone buzzed. A man in the back coughed, too loud, too late.

Adrian’s eyes gleamed. “Our new collection, *Rupture*, debuts in seven days. It is entirely ours. Entirely *new*. History isn’t rewritten. It’s built.”

Nell extended a pen.

The first buyer to sign would be the first to betray.

A woman in a red coat approached, her heels clicking like impatient heels. She signed without hesitation. The pen scratched the paper with finality.

Nell flipped the page. Another sheet. Another signature.

Moreau Atelier was dying in real time.

Vivienne stood at the back of the room, framed by the archway, her black turtleneck blending into the shadows. She held a manila envelope in one hand, her knuckles pale as old lace. No one noticed her. No one ever did.

Damien Laurent, the journalist from *R fashion*, stood beside her, his camera slung low. He didn’t look at her. He watched the signatures fall.

“You could stop this,” he murmured.

“No,” she said.

“You could interrupt.”

“It would only make it worse.”

“Typical Moreau pride,” he said, but there was no bite in it.

She didn’t rise to it. Her gaze stayed fixed on Nell as the final sheets were signed, folded, passed to assistants who carried them to a printer in the corner. The contracts were already in triplicate: one for the lawyers, one for the accountants, one for the shredder.

Julian was there too, leaning against a side pillar, arms crossed. His sleeves were rolled to the elbow, exposing forearms dusted with ink stains. He watched the proceedings with the quiet intensity of a man who understood the cost of silence.

Vivienne had taught him that.

Nell finished. She closed the portfolio with a snap. The sound cracked through the room like a gunshot.

Applause broke out, tentative at first, then swelling into something hollow and predictable. Adrian raised his hands in mock gratitude. Nell’s smile never wavered.

Vivienne turned.

She walked toward the exit, her heels silent on the polished concrete. Damien followed, his shoes squeaking on the waxed floor.

Julian pushed away from the pillar. He fell into step beside Vivienne, his shoulder brushing hers as they reached the door.

She didn’t flinch.

“You should’ve stopped her,” he said, voice low.

Vivienne didn’t look at him. “I did.”

“You signed the dissolution.”

“Of the label. Not of the truth.”

He exhaled through his nose. “Adrian’s using your patterns. Your stitches. Your goddamn handwriting on the margin notes.”

“Yes.”

“You let him.”

“I let him take what he thought was given.”

Julian’s jaw tightened. “That’s not how contracts work.”

“Contracts are written by those who hold the scissors,” she said.

He stopped walking. She kept going.

Damien paused beside Julian, his camera dangling. “You know her?”

Julian didn’t answer. He watched Vivienne disappear into the February chill outside.

Nell’s phone buzzed in her pocket.

She pulled it out. A message lit the screen:

*You always were a better student than you knew.*

Adrian appeared beside her, his smile still frozen in place. “What’s that?”

She showed him.

His brow furrowed. “Who sent it?”

Nell swallowed. “Unknown.”

He laughed, bright and brittle. “Vivienne playing phantom queen? Classic diva exit.”

Nell slipped the phone back into her coat. She didn’t laugh.

The champagne arrived at the Four Seasons as planned.

Adrian held a flute aloft, ice clinking against crystal. “To the future.”

Glasses rose. The room echoed with toasted ambition.

Nell didn’t drink. She stared at her phone, at the single unanswered message.

Vivienne’s thumb hovered over *send*. The screen glowed like a warning.

*Retrieve the vault key from your safe. Now.*

Chapter 3

The Greene Street atelier smelled of aged paper and lemon oil. Dust motes hung in the weak winter light slanting through the high studio windows. Vivienne sat at her drafting table, fingers resting on the edge of an uncut bolt of silk the color of smoked quartz. The space was empty except for the ghost of work past—mannequins wrapped in muslin shrouds, scissors hung in perfect rows, a half-finished toile draped over a dummy like a shroud.

Her phone buzzed on the table. Damien Laurent’s name flashed across the screen. She didn’t move.

The call ended. A minute later, another. Then a third. The phone vibrated against the wood, insistent as a woodpecker.

She opened the top drawer. Removed a pair of steel shears. Clicked them open. Clicked them closed. The sound filled the silence.

The phone fell silent.

Footsteps echoed in the stairwell outside. Light. Hesitant. A key turned in the lock.

Vivienne didn’t turn around.

The door creaked open. Eleanor Whitmore stood in the doorway, wrapped in a charcoal wool coat, her usual confidence clipped at the edges. She held a leather portfolio in one hand and a bottle of champagne in the other, its foil dimpled with condensation. The label read *Dom Pérignon 2012*.

“You’re not answering your phone,” Nell said.

Vivienne didn’t look up. “I’m working.”

Nell stepped inside. The door swung shut behind her. “You’re brooding. There’s a difference.”

“I prefer *processing*.”

Nell set the bottle on the drafting table. Ice clinked against glass. “Adrian sent this. Said you’d appreciate the vintage.”

Vivienne finally turned her head. Her eyes were sharp, unblinking. “Does he think I’m crying into a glass of this?”

Nell unscrewed the cage, popped the cork. The report was crisp, precise. No theatrics. “He thinks you’re enjoying the view from the sidelines.”

Vivienne’s lips curved, thin and humorless. “I own the view.”

Nell poured two glasses. The champagne frothed, golden and wasteful. “Do you?”

A beat. Vivienne’s gaze dropped to the portfolio Nell clutched like a shield. “You signed the dissolution papers today.”

Nell froze. “Yes.”

“You signed away Moreau Atelier.”

“It was time.”

“Was it?”

Nell exhaled through her nose. She took a sip of champagne, winced as if the taste were an insult. “You had your chance to stop it. You didn’t.”

“I didn’t have to sign anything.”

Nell set her glass down too hard. Liquid sloshed over the rim, staining the portfolio’s leather. “You *did*. You signed the contract giving Adrian full control. You walked out. You let us rewrite the history.”

Vivienne reached for the portfolio. Nell pulled it back.

“You don’t get to touch it,” Nell said. ‘Not anymore.”

Vivienne held out her hand. “I designed every piece in there. Every stitch. Every goddamn buttonhole.”

“And Adrian designed the new collection. Using your sketches, your patterns, your *words* in the margins.” Nell’s voice cracked. Just once. “Julian even found your discarded scraps in the trash.”

Vivienne’s fingers twitched. “Julian shouldn’t be digging through my trash.”

“He’s not. He was cleaning up after a late night. Found your initials on a sleeve pattern. Realized where Adrian’s genius really came from.” Nell’s eyes burned. “You let him take it all. You let him *become* you.”

Vivienne stood. The chair scraped against the floor, a sound like a blade dragged across bone. She walked to the far wall. Pressed a palm against the cool plaster. A seam was hidden beneath the paint—a narrow door, disguised as wainscoting. Her fingers found the hidden latch. A click. The panel swung inward, revealing a shallow alcove.

Inside, a single key glinted on a hook.

Nell’s breath hitched. “You keep the vault key here? In your *atelier*?”

Vivienne turned. “I keep it where no one looks.”

Nell’s champagne glass slipped from her fingers. It shattered on the hardwood floor. The sound was loud, final. Neither woman flinched.

“You don’t understand,” Nell said, voice low. “Adrian didn’t just take your name. He took your *clients*. Your suppliers. Your goddamn reputation. The Moreau Atelier clientele bought Voss & Co dresses at the after-parties tonight. They signed contracts with us before the ink was dry on your resignation.”

Vivienne stepped closer. The scent of bergamot and old paper rose between them. “Do they know who they’re wearing?”

Nell’s jaw tightened. “They wear what’s presented. They wear the future.”

Vivienne reached into the alcove. Her fingers closed around the key. Cold seeped through her skin.

“You’re wrong,” she said.

Nell’s voice dropped to a whisper. “About what?”

Vivienne held up the key. It dangled between them, a promise and a threat. “I never signed away everything.”

Nell’s eyes flickered to the key, then back to Vivienne’s face. Something shifted in her expression—doubt, fear, the flicker of a memory long buried. “That’s impossible. I handled the dissolution. I reviewed the contracts. You signed the final page.”

Vivienne’s smile was a knife edge. “Did I?”

Nell’s portfolio fell to the floor. Papers scattered like startled birds. She didn’t notice. Her focus was locked on Vivienne’s hand, the key, the implication.

A sound from the stairwell.

Footsteps. Fast. Light.

Julian Cross appeared in the doorway, his dark hair tousled, his sleeves rolled to the elbows. Ink stains tracked up his forearms like tattoos. His eyes flicked between them—Nell’s panic, Vivienne’s stillness, the shattered glass at their feet.

He held a manila folder in one hand. His knuckles were white.

“Nell,” he said, voice rough. “Adrian wants the new line samples moved to the showroom. Now.”

Nell didn’t move. Her gaze never left Vivienne.

Julian took a step forward. His shoulder brushed the drafting table. The champagne bottle wobbled. He steadied it with a flat palm.

Vivienne watched his hand. Watched the way his fingers curled, steadying, commanding. Familiar.

“You,” she said.

Julian looked at her. Really looked. Not the Vivienne Moreau of legend. Not the ghost in the shadows. The woman who had taught him the difference between a backstitch and a fell stitch. The woman who had let him cry in her office when his mother died.

“You let him take your work,” he said.

Vivienne didn’t answer.

Nell finally broke the silence. “Julian, get the samples. Now.”

He didn’t move. “Why are you here?”

Nell’s voice was a whipcrack. “This isn’t your concern.”

Julian’s eyes stayed on Vivienne. “Isn’t it?”

Vivienne held up the key. “Retrieve the vault key from your safe. Now.”

Nell’s face went pale. Her breath came fast, shallow.

Julian’s gaze flicked to the key. Then to Nell.

Then back to Vivienne.

The light in the atelier dimmed. The city outside hummed, indifferent. Somewhere, a siren wailed, then faded.

Nell’s phone buzzed in her coat pocket. A vibration, sharp and insistent.

Vivienne didn’t look away from Julian.

Julian didn’t look away from Vivienne.

The phone buzzed again.

The silence between them was a living thing—heavy, electric, alive with everything unsaid.

Nell pulled out her phone. Her thumbs hovered over the screen.

Then she turned on her heel and walked out, the key still clutched in Vivienne’s hand burning between them like a brand.

Chapter 4

The Four Seasons ballroom smelled of citrus and expensive perfume, the air thick with the kind of laughter that cost ten thousand dollars a year to fake. Adrian Voss stood at the center of it all, one hand draped over a crystal flute like it was a woman he’d already decided he could discard. The CFDA nomination had just been announced—*Voss & Co: Finalist for International Designer of the Year*—and the room pulsed with the kind of triumph that makes champagne taste like victory.

Nell stood beside him, chin lifted, fingers curled around her own glass like she was measuring the weight of her betrayal. Every so often, she checked her phone, her thumb hovering over the screen as if she expected a text that might save her. It never came.

Across the room, a buyer from Milan—someone Vivienne had once dressed personally—lifted a glass in Adrian’s direction. “Your spring line is exquisite,” she said. “So fresh. So *you*.”

Adrian’s smile didn’t waver. “Thank you, Daniela. It’s about time the world caught up.”

Nell’s grip tightened. The buyer either didn’t notice or didn’t care.

A journalist from *W* sidled up, notepad out. “Adrian, any truth to the rumors that Moreau Atelier’s decline was—”

Adrian cut him off with a laugh. “Rumors? Darling, Vivienne Moreau stepped away to focus on private commissions. A very wise move.” He took a sip, let the lie settle. “She’s happy. We’re happy. Everyone’s happy.”

Nell’s phone buzzed. She glanced down, then excused herself without a word, weaving through the crowd toward the lobby. Adrian’s eyes followed her, but only for a second. The room was his now.

Julian stood near the bar, arms crossed, watching the dancers. He hadn’t touched his drink. His sleeves were rolled to the elbows, the cuffs loose, the kind of careless elegance that made him look like he belonged anywhere and nowhere. When Nell passed him, she didn’t acknowledge him. But her fingers brushed his wrist—a silent signal. A warning.

He didn’t react. Just turned back to the room and caught Adrian’s eye.

Adrian raised his glass in salute. Julian lifted his untouched drink in return.

Then he moved through the crowd, closer to the center, where the light was brighter and the lies were thicker.

A woman in a sequined dress blocked his path, her smile sharp as a knife. “Julian Cross. I didn’t think you’d show.”

He recognized her—buyer from Bergdorf’s, one of Nell’s favorites. “Had to see the man of the hour.”

She laughed, a sound like breaking glass. “Oh, he’s *something* alright.” Her gaze flicked to Adrian, who was now holding court near the piano. “You working on the new line?”

Julian kept his face neutral. “Always.”

“Funny.” She leaned in, her perfume cloying. “I heard a rumor that pieces from Moreau Atelier’s archives ended up in Adrian’s collection.”

Julian’s fingers twitched. “Is that so?”

She nodded, eyes gleaming. “Vivienne Moreau’s signature stitching. The Moreau blue. It’s all there.” She took a sip of her drink. “Strange that no one seems to care where it came from.”

Julian didn’t answer. His gaze drifted over the room, landing on Nell as she stood near the elevators, phone pressed to her ear. She was pale. Nodding. Listening.

Then her eyes flicked up, meeting his across the room.

Something passed between them—fear, maybe. Or recognition.

She turned away first.

Julian exhaled through his nose. The air in the ballroom felt suddenly heavy, like the room itself was holding its breath.

Adrian clapped a hand on his shoulder. “Looking sharp, Julian. You always do.” His voice was low, private. “The new line is almost ready. Just a few tweaks on the drape of the trousers. You’ll handle it?”

Julian nodded. “Of course.”

Adrian grinned, white teeth flashing. “Good man.” He glanced around, then lowered his voice. “Nell tells me you found some… interesting sketches in the trash.”

Julian’s pulse jumped. “Just scraps.”

“Scraps that looked like Moreau?” Adrian’s smile didn’t waver, but his grip on Julian’s shoulder tightened. “Funny how those things find their way to you.”

Julian kept his voice steady. “Must be a coincidence.”

Adrian studied him for a long moment, then clapped him on the shoulder again, harder this time. “Coincidences aren’t the kind of thing we reward around here.”

He moved on, leaving Julian standing there, the weight of the moment pressing down on him.

Julian made his way to the bar. Ordered a whiskey. Drank it in one swallow. The burn grounded him.

A hand touched his elbow. He turned.

A woman stood there, her dark hair pulled into a tight chignon, her dress a simple black sheath that looked expensive and understated. Her eyes were sharp, assessing.

“You’re Julian Cross,” she said.

He didn’t answer. Waited.

“I’m Clara Reyes.” She held out a business card. *R fashion*. “I was hoping we could talk.”

Julian took the card. Didn’t look at it. “About?”

Clara smiled, but it wasn’t warm. “Vivienne Moreau.”

The name hit him like a punch to the gut. He pocketed the card without a word.

Clara’s smile widened. “I’ll take that as a yes.”

She walked away before he could respond, leaving Julian standing in the middle of the room, the noise of the party crashing around him.

Nell’s phone buzzed again. She answered it, her voice a whisper. “Yes?”

A pause. Then her face went still.

Julian watched her. Something was wrong.

She ended the call. Looked up. Saw him watching.

Their eyes locked.

Then she turned and walked away, her heels clicking against the marble like a metronome counting down to something irreversible.

Julian didn’t move.

The party raged on around him, the laughter and clinking glasses a cacophony of lies.

But all he could hear was the echo of Vivienne’s name.

Chapter 5

The Voss & Co showroom on Mercer Street was a cathedral of steel and glass, all sharp angles and harsh light. Adrian stood at the center of it, arms crossed, watching his design team scramble. The problem wasn’t the sketches. It was the fabric.

“It’s not fitting right,” said Lila, the head of production, her voice tight. “The drape is wrong. The weight—it’s like it’s fighting the cut.”

Adrian didn’t look at her. “Then fix it.”

“Adrian, the measurements are off by half an inch in the bodice. Moreau Atelier stitching doesn’t work like this.”

A beat of silence. Adrian’s jaw clenched.

Julian crossed the room, his hands shoved in his pockets, sleeves rolled past his elbows. He didn’t wait to be acknowledged. He picked up a sleeve from the cutting table, ran his fingers along the stitching. His brow furrowed.

“These aren’t standard Voss & Co,” he said.

Adrian finally turned. “Of course they’re not. They’re *new*.”

“No.” Julian held up the fabric. “These are Moreau.”

The room stilled. Even the hum of the overhead lights seemed to pause.

Adrian stepped forward, plucked the sleeve from Julian’s hands. Examined it. “What?”

“Look at the understitching.” Julian pointed. “The Moreau blue. The tension. It’s not our technique.”

Adrian’s grip tightened. “Where the hell did this come from?”

Julian didn’t answer right away. He moved to the trash bin near the cutting station, lifted the lid. Rummaged through the scraps—swatches, pattern paper, half-burned sketches. His fingers stilled.

He pulled out a piece of paper, singed at the edges, the ink smudged but legible. A Moreau silhouette. The stitch marks around the hem were familiar. Too familiar.

Julian held it up. “This was in the trash.”

Adrian’s face darkened. “That was garbage.”

“You threw out Moreau sketches?”

“They were old. Outdated.” Adrian’s voice was sharp. “What’s your point?”

Julian didn’t flinch. “My point is these aren’t outdated. They’re *recent*.”

A vein in Adrian’s temple pulsed. “You’re imagining things.”

Julian set the sketch on the table, smoothed it out. “I’ve worked with Vivienne Moreau’s patterns for years. I know her stitching when I see it.”

Adrian picked up the sketch, tore it in half. Dropped the pieces back into the bin. “There. Problem solved.”

Julian exhaled through his nose. “That’s not the point.”

“What’s the point, Julian?” Adrian’s voice dropped, low and dangerous. “You questioning my judgment?”

Julian met his gaze. Held it. “I’m saying we have a problem.”

Adrian stared at him for a long moment. Then he turned, strode to the window, and stared out at the street below. Mercer was quiet this time of day, the usual traffic replaced by couriers and the occasional black car.

“You’re overreacting,” Adrian said finally.

Julian didn’t respond.

Adrian turned back. “Fine. Then fix it.”

“Fix what?”

“The *problem*.” Adrian’s voice was a whipcrack. “You’re my lead pattern cutter. Figure it out.”

Julian didn’t move. “The fabric’s wrong. The stitching’s wrong. The whole fucking collection is wrong.”

Adrian’s jaw tightened. “Then use your magic hands and make it right.”

Julian’s fingers curled into fists. “I’m not a magician.”

“No.” Adrian’s smile was razor-thin. “You’re just my employee.”

Julian held his gaze for another beat. Then he turned, grabbed his toolkit from the shelf, and walked out without another word.

The door clicked shut behind him.

Adrian stared after him, then turned to Lila. “Get me more fabric. *Real* fabric. Not whatever garbage Julian’s poking at.”

Lila nodded, already reaching for her phone. “On it.”

Adrian grabbed his coat from the back of a chair, shrugged it on. “I’ve got a meeting. Deal with it.”

He left without waiting for a response.

---

Julian stepped into the stairwell, the metal door slamming shut behind him. He took the stairs two at a time, the echo of his boots bouncing off the concrete. His hands were steady, but his mind wasn’t.

He pulled out his phone, scrolled to his contacts. Stopped at Clara Reyes.

He typed a message.

*We need to talk. Now.*

He hit send before he could second-guess himself.

The phone buzzed immediately. Clara’s reply.

*Meet me at the café on Prince. Ten minutes.*

Julian tucked the phone away. Took the stairs faster.

The café was a narrow, dim-lit space wedged between a bodega and a vintage record store. The kind of place where the espresso was strong and the Wi-Fi was nonexistent. Clara was already there, seated at a corner table, a laptop open in front of her. She didn’t look up as Julian approached.

He slid into the chair across from her.

Clara finally glanced up. “Took you long enough.”

Julian didn’t sit back. Didn’t relax. “You knew.”

She closed the laptop. “Knew what?”

“About the sketches. About Moreau.”

Clara leaned back, arms crossed. “I know a lot of things.”

Julian’s knee bounced under the table. “Why mention it at the party?”

“Because you looked like a man who might be useful.” She tilted her head. “And you *are* useful, aren’t you? Lead pattern cutter. Adrian’s right hand. His favorite errand boy.”

Julian’s fingers dug into the edge of the table. “I didn’t sign up to be anyone’s pawn.”

Clara smiled. “No. But you did sign up to work for a thief.”

Julian went very still.

Clara reached into her bag, pulled out a folded sheet of paper. Slid it across the table.

Julian didn’t touch it.

“That’s a copy of the Moreau Atelier client list,” Clara said. “Every name, every order, every account that kept the lights on in that atelier for twenty years.” She tapped the paper. “Adrian took it. Lock, stock, and barrel.”

Julian’s stomach twisted. “You’re lying.”

Clara’s smile didn’t waver. “Am I?” She leaned forward. “Ask yourself this: Why would Adrian throw out sketches that look like Moreau’s if he didn’t want to forget? If he didn’t *need* to forget?”

Julian’s throat went dry.

Clara stood, slung her bag over her shoulder. “Think about it. Then call me.”

She walked out, leaving Julian alone with the paper and the weight of what he knew.

He unfolded it.

At the top was a name.

His sister’s.

Nell Whitmore.

The paper slipped from his fingers. Landed on the table with a soft thud.

His phone buzzed again. A message from Nell.

*Julian. Where are you? We have a problem.*

Chapter 6

The Greene Street atelier smelled of lavender and old paper. Vivienne stood at her desk, fingers hovering over the keyboard, the glow of the screen the only light in the room. Her hands didn’t tremble. They never did.

Outside, the February wind rattled the fire escape. She ignored it.

A single email open on her desktop. Subject: *Urgent – Fabric Recall.*

She hit send.

Then she closed the laptop.

---

At Voss & Co, Adrian’s phone buzzed on the quartz console in his Mercer Street office. He didn’t look at it. Not immediately. He was watching his reflection in the floor-to-ceiling windows, adjusting the knot on his Brunello Cucinelli tie.

Then his screen flashed.

A push alert from *R fashion.*

He tapped it.

The headline burned across the screen: *MOREAU ATTELIER FABRICS VANISH FROM VOS CO SUPPLY CHAIN.*

Adrian’s breath stopped.

Nell leaned in, read over his shoulder. Her face went pale.

“This isn’t a rival,” she said quietly. “This is *her.*”

Adrian’s phone rang. Unknown number. He answered.

“Voss.”

Silence. Then a voice, soft but clear.

“Did you miss me?”

The line went dead.

Adrian’s grip tightened. The phone cracked in his hand.

Chapter 7

The private preview took place in a seventh-floor loft on Hudson Street, all exposed brick and track lighting. Adrian stood beneath a spotlight, his hands clasped behind his back, smiling the smile he’d practiced in front of a mirror that morning. The buyers moved in a slow orbit around the runway, murmuring approval, lifting swatches to the light, nodding like worshippers.

A woman in a navy trench coat stepped forward. No ring light, no phone out to capture the moment—just her, her purse clutched at her waist, her heels sinking slightly into the rug.

“Adrian,” she said.

He didn’t look at her. “Lydia. Thank you for coming.”

“I’m here every season,” she said. “But this time, something’s missing.”

Adrian finally turned. The smile stayed, but his eyes tightened at the corners. “We’ve been refining the collection for months.”

“Not the collection.” Lydia’s voice was quiet, but the people around them stopped pretending to examine the garments. “The Moreau drape. It’s gone.”

Silence spread like a stain. Adrian could feel heat creeping up his collar.

He stepped toward her, close enough to smell her perfume—something expensive, something he couldn’t place. “Vivienne Moreau is retired. Her aesthetic is—”

“Inspired designers,” Lydia cut in. “I know. But no one drapes like her. The way the fabric falls, the way it *lives* on the body—your new line doesn’t have it. It’s tailored, Adrian. It’s *built*. Not *worn*.”

Adrian’s fingers twitched at his sides. He glanced at the exit, then back at Lydia. “You’re welcome to schedule a private appointment. One of the stylists can show you—”

“I don’t want a stylist.” Lydia’s voice sharpened. “I want to know why the woman who built this brand is suddenly vapor. I want to know why no one’s talking about it.”

A beat. Two.

Adrian’s jaw tightened. “You’ll have to ask the board.”

Lydia didn’t move. “I *did*. Nell said there was ‘a restructuring.’ But restructuring doesn’t erase history.”

Adrian exhaled through his nose. “Lydia, if you’re concerned about continuity, we can discuss future collaborations with Moreau Atelier’s former clients. Many of them have already shown interest.”

Lydia’s mouth thinned. “You mean your clients now.”

“Precisely.”

She stared at him for a long moment, then turned on her heel and walked away, her coat swinging behind her like a cape. The buzz of conversation resumed, but it was thinner now, brittle.

Adrian didn’t watch her go. He walked instead, pushing through the crowd, ignoring the murmurs that followed him. He took the stairs to the seventh floor, his polished shoes clicking against the metal treads, each step a countdown.

Nell’s office door was closed. He knocked once, then pushed it open without waiting for an answer.

She sat behind her desk, fingers paused over her keyboard, her dark eyes flicking up with practiced calm.

“Adrian.”

“She’s gone,” he said.

Nell didn’t ask who. She didn’t have to.

“Lydia Reyes just asked why Moreau’s gone.”

Nell exhaled through her nose. “She’s not the only one.”

“No.” Adrian strode to the window, hands on his hips. Below, the Hudson glittered under sodium lights. “She’s the only one who said it out loud.”

Nell reached for her phone. “I’ll call the PR team. We’ll spin—”

“There’s no spin.” Adrian turned, his voice low and dangerous. “She’s out there. Watching. Waiting.”

Nell’s phone buzzed. She glanced at the screen, then froze.

Adrian saw the color drain from her face.

“What?” he demanded.

Nell looked up. “It’s not a call.”

She held out a sealed envelope.

White. Unmarked. No stamp. No return address.

Adrian didn’t take it.

Nell set it on the desk between them.

“It’s addressed to Vivienne Moreau.”

The name hung in the air like a knife.

Adrian’s breath stopped.

Nell’s fingers trembled as she pushed the envelope toward him. “It just came up. From the front desk.”

Adrian stared at it. Then at Nell.

“Open it,” he said.

Nell didn’t move. “You open it.”

Adrian picked it up. His name wasn’t on it. Vivienne’s was. Bold, typed, no frills.

He tore the flap.

A single sheet of paper, heavy stock. At the top, the Moreau Atelier crest—subtle, embossed in gold.

Below it, three words in block letters:

*You stole from me.*

Adrian’s hand clenched around the paper. His pulse roared in his ears.

Nell reached for it.

He snatched it back.

“This isn’t her,” he said. “This is a prank. A stunt. Someone trying to rattle us before Fashion Week.”

Nell didn’t answer. She was staring at the desk.

Adrian followed her gaze.

A second envelope lay there, smaller, sleek. Black. His name printed in sharp, modern font.

He didn’t touch it.

Nell did.

She slit it open. Pulled out a single Polaroid.

A photograph of a dress pattern taped to a cutting table. Vivienne’s handwriting on the margins. The date was yesterday.

Adrian’s stomach dropped.

Nell’s breath hitched.

Outside the office, someone laughed. A sharp, bright sound, too loud in the tense air.

Adrian’s phone buzzed again. He fumbled it from his pocket.

Unknown number.

He answered.

“Voss.”

Silence.

Then a woman’s voice, low and amused.

“Check your mail.”

The line went dead.

Adrian lowered the phone.

Nell was already at the door, her coat in hand.

“Where are you going?” he demanded.

She didn’t look back. “To find out who just mailed us Vivienne Moreau.”

Chapter 8

The office smelled like stale coffee and adrenaline. Nell stood by the window, phone pressed to her ear, her reflection fractured in the rain-streaked glass. Outside, a black town car idled at the curb, door open.

*I know what you did to her. I know what you did to me.*

She handed it to Adrian.

He read it. His face went white.

Outside the office, the sound of raised voices cut through the tension. The design team, clustered near the elevators, arguing over fabric swatches. One of them—a junior stylist—held up a Moreau Atelier label tag and shook it.

Adrian saw it. His pupils contracted.

Nell’s phone buzzed again. She didn’t check it.

Adrian moved to the door. “I’m calling security. We end this now.”

Nell didn’t stop him.

*#VossGate trending. Sources say Voss stole designs from silent partner Vivienne Moreau.*

Adrian didn’t look at it. He strode toward the stairs, passing the young man without a word. The stylist’s eyes widened. He lowered the phone.

Her phone lit up again. A single word flashed on the screen:

*Proof.*

Nell’s finger hovered over the screen.

The phone buzzed again.

This time, the screen showed a photo.

A cutting table. A dress form. A pair of scissors on the floor.

And on the table, half-cut into a pattern, was a Moreau Atelier label.

Nell’s stomach lurched.

The photo was timestamped.

*Today. 2:47 p.m.*

She looked up.

Julian stood at the far end of the hall, his hands in his pockets, his expression unreadable.

He raised a hand.

Just once.

Then he turned and walked away.

Chapter 9

The bell above the Greene Street door chimed. Vivienne didn’t look up from her tea tray. The porcelain clinked against the silver, steam curling into the sharp February air.

Nell stepped inside, her coat still damp at the hem. She didn’t shake it off. The atelier smelled of starch and old wood, of charcoal sketches left too long in the light.

“You’re late,” Vivienne said.

Nell’s boots clicked on the polished floor. “Traffic from the Garment District.”

Vivienne poured. The teapot’s spout didn’t wobble. “You always were a terrible liar, Eleanor.”

Nell sat without invitation. The chair groaned under her weight. “I didn’t come to fight.”

“Then why did you come?” Vivienne pushed the cup toward her. The saucer had a hairline crack along the rim—one Vivienne had never bothered to repair.

Nell exhaled. “You know why.”

Vivienne lifted the lid from her own cup, let the aroma rise before answering. “Tell me anyway.”

“To offer you something.” Nell’s fingers tapped the armrest. A designer’s tell. “Ten percent of Voss & Co. Voting shares. A seat on the board. A name in the credits.”

Vivienne set the lid down. “And if I refuse?”

“Then this escalates.” Nell’s voice stayed level. “You know how it goes. Lawyers. Auditors. The kind of scrutiny that leaves a brand looking like a house of cards.”

Vivienne sipped. The tea was strong enough to stain. “You’re threatening me with *good taste*?”

Nell’s jaw tightened. “I’m telling you where this ends if you don’t take the out.”

Vivienne set the cup down. The porcelain made a soft, final sound. “Where does it end, Nell?”

“With you fading out.” Nell’s gaze dropped to the table. A single sheet of paper lay facedown between them. “With Adrian taking the CFDA. With Voss & Co becoming a legacy brand instead of a flash in the pan. With you remembered as a footnote—if you’re remembered at all.”

Vivienne didn’t touch the paper. “And if I take your offer?”

“Then you walk away with a fortune. No more lawsuits. No more whispers.” Nell leaned forward. “You can start fresh. Somewhere warm. Somewhere quiet.”

Vivienne studied Nell’s face—the freckles she’d tried to bleach out, the shadows under her eyes that ran deeper than jet lag. “You believe that?”

Nell didn’t blink. “I believe in self-preservation.”

A silence settled. Outside, a delivery truck rumbled past, shaking the front window’s stained glass into fractured light.

Vivienne reached for the envelope. Slid it across.

Nell didn’t open it. She knew what it was. The severance agreement. The one she’d signed. The one that erased Vivienne Moreau from the story.

“Burn it,” Vivienne said.

Nell’s fingers hovered over the flap. “This isn’t a negotiation.”

“No.” Vivienne’s voice was soft, almost gentle. “It’s a surrender.”

Nell finally opened the envelope. Inside was a single sheet, crisp and new. She unfolded it. The ink was Vivienne’s—looped and precise.

Nell’s breath stilled.

The document was a certified copy of the Moreau Atelier incorporation papers.

Dated 2009.

Signed by Adrian Voss.

Nell’s hands trembled. “Where did you get this?”

Vivienne didn’t answer. She stood, walked to the drafting table, and lifted a yellowed sketch—Adrian’s earliest collection, the one that launched him. Beneath it was a file.

She brought it back. Dropped it on the table between them.

Nell opened it. Blueprints. Contracts. Client lists. Every stitch of Moreau’s legacy before Adrian ever picked up a pencil.

Nell’s voice cracked. “You kept all of this?”

Vivienne returned to her seat. “I built it. He stole it. But the law remembers what the law sees.” She tapped the papers. “These say you signed my name away. Not the other way around.”

Nell’s throat worked. “You’re not blackmailing me.”

“I’m reminding you.” Vivienne folded her hands. “Ten percent isn’t enough to make this go away. Not for either of us.”

Nell’s phone buzzed in her coat pocket. She ignored it.

“What do you want, then?” Nell asked.

Vivienne smiled. The first real smile Nell had seen in years. “I don’t want a seat at your table.”

She reached across and slid the incorporation papers toward Nell. The corner crinkled under Nell’s wrist.

“Take it,” Vivienne said. “Keep it. Burn it. I don’t care.”

Nell’s phone buzzed again. She pulled it out. A text from an unknown number:

*Check the security feed at Mercer Street. You’re about to lose more than designs.*

She looked up. Vivienne was already standing, gathering the teacups with slow, deliberate care.

Nell stood too. “This isn’t over.”

Vivienne turned. Her eyes were clear. “It ended when you signed the papers, Eleanor.”

Nell’s fingers clenched around her phone. “You don’t have the stomach for this.”

Vivienne walked to the window. Looked out at Greene Street, where the afternoon light slanted through the bare trees.

“Stomach?” She spoke over her shoulder. “No. But I have a notebook. And a list. And a journalist who’s very interested in the gaps in your accounting.”

Nell’s breath caught. She knew that tone. It was the same one Vivienne used before a fitting—the quiet before the storm.

Vivienne turned back. “Tell Adrian I said hello.”

Nell didn’t answer. She walked to the door. The bell chimed again, hollow and final.

Vivienne waited until the door closed. Then she walked to her desk, opened the bottom drawer, and pulled out a burner phone.

She dialed. Waited.

“Damien,” she said. “We need to talk about the scope of the story.”

Chapter 10

The café smelled of espresso and damp wool, the kind of place where journalists went to die quietly. Damien Laurent sat in the back corner, elbows on a chipped Formica table, sleeves rolled to the elbows. A legal pad lay open, a single line written in blue ink like a scar across the page.

Vivienne slid into the opposite chair. The vinyl creaked. Her coat brushed his knee under the table.

Damien looked up. His eyes were the color of old newsprint—flat, unreadable. “You’re late.”

Vivienne set her bag on the floor. “I don’t do late. I do on time.”

He tapped the legal pad. “I’ve got seven minutes.”

“Good.” She folded her hands. “Because I’m not selling a story. I’m selling you a revolution.”

Damien’s pen hovered over the page. “Try me.”

“Adrian Voss isn’t the creative genius he claims to be.” Vivienne’s voice dropped, measured. “He’s a thief. A plagiarist. A man who built an empire by standing on the shoulders of a woman he erased.”

Damien leaned back. The chair groaned. “You’re saying you’re Vivienne Moreau.”

“I’m saying I’m the woman who signed the checks.” Vivienne reached into her bag. Laid a manila folder on the table. Inside were photocopies—bank deposits, wire transfers, invoices addressed to Adrian Voss in the early years.

Damien didn’t touch them. “And what do you get out of this?”

“Everything.” The word hung between them, heavy as a stone. “The CFDA award. The controlling stake in Voss & Co. The proper credit for a lifetime of work.”

Damien flipped the pen between his fingers. “You want me to break a story that destroys Adrian Voss. What’s my angle?”

“You’re a journalist.” Vivienne met his gaze. “You find the truth. You write the truth. You let the world decide.”

“And if the world doesn’t care?”

Vivienne smiled. It wasn’t warm. “Then you’ll have written the first draft of history. And history always remembers the footnotes.”

Damien exhaled through his nose. “You’re asking for a lot.”

“I’m offering you a legacy.” Vivienne slid the folder closer. “All the documents are in here—proof of every payment, every contract, every lie Adrian told to bury me. The clients I brought. The designs I sketched. The brand I built.”

Damien opened the folder. His thumb brushed a photocopy of a 2015 deposit slip. Moreau Atelier, Investor Capital. Fifteen million dollars.

His breath stilled.

Vivienne watched his face. “That was Adrian’s first collection. The one that made him. Paid for by a woman with no name.”

Damien closed the folder. His jaw tightened. “Why now?”

“Because Adrian thinks he’s won.” Vivienne’s fingers threaded together. “He’s rewritten the history books. Tonight he’ll toast his CFDA nomination. Tomorrow he’ll unveil a collection built on my stolen work. And in seven days he’ll float Voss & Co as the next great American luxury brand.”

Damien’s pen pressed into the legal pad. “And you?”

“I spent ten years watching my name disappear from the credits.” Vivienne’s voice didn’t rise, didn’t waver. “I’m not watching my designs walk down a runway without me.”

A silence settled. The espresso machine hissed. A barista laughed at the counter, high and brittle.

Damien capped his pen. “What’s your endgame?”

Vivienne smiled. Quiet. Final.

“A fashion week show.”

The words landed like a gavel.

Damien’s pen slipped from his fingers. Rolled off the table, clattered onto the floor. He didn’t pick it up.

“You’re serious.”

Vivienne gathered her bag. “I’m methodical.”

Damien rubbed his temple. “You realize this isn’t just a story. This is a war.”

Vivienne stood. The chair scraped. “I didn’t start it. But I’ll finish it.”

She turned toward the door. Then paused.

“Damien.”

He looked up.

“Find Julian Cross.” She dropped a business card on the table. A number written in pencil on the back. “He’s the pattern cutter. He knows where the bodies are buried. Literally.”

Damien picked up the card. His thumb smudged the ink.

Vivienne walked out. The bell above the door chimed once, sharp as a gunshot.

Chapter 11

The Mercer Street showroom was a tomb of marble and chrome after hours. Adrian stood at the center of the space, his reflection fractured across the floor-to-ceiling mirrors, his jaw clenched so tight his molars hummed. Nell’s phone buzzed. She fumbled for it, her fingers trembling. Read the screen. Her breath hitched.

Adrian snatched the phone from her hand.

*Husband filed for divorce. Assets frozen. I’m ruined. And it’s all your fault. You should have listened to me.*

The message was from Eleanor Whitmore Sr.

Adrian’s hands shook. He tossed the phone back. It skittered across the table, teetering on the edge before falling to the floor.

Outside, the sleet had turned to snow. The city lights blurred through the studio windows, a haze of gold and white.

Julian picked up the button. Held it between his fingers. “She’s not done with you.”

Adrian didn’t answer.

He didn’t have to.

The studio was too quiet.

And then—

A soft *thud* from upstairs.

Like a body hitting the floor.

Chapter 12

The Lincoln Center backstage area smelled of hairspray and adrenaline. Mirrors lined the walls, each reflecting a different version of the same chaos—models in various stages of undress, stylists wielding mascara wands like weapons, a PA shouting into a headset about a missing heel.

Vivienne stood at the center of it all, her back to the mirror. Nell Whitmore watched from the doorway, her posture rigid, her lips pressed into a thin line. She carried a tablet under one arm, her fingers twitching against the edge.

“You’re late,” Vivienne said without turning.

Nell didn’t answer. She stepped into the light. The glow of overhead fluorescents caught the diamond stud in her left ear—a piece Vivienne had given her on her first day at Moreau Atelier.

“You look like her,” Vivienne added.

Nell’s hand went to her ear. A gesture, a shield. “I chose it.”

Vivienne turned. The gown caught the light—satin and silk, a cascade of silver and black, a silhouette Vivienne had sketched in the margins of a napkin ten years ago. The neckline dipped low, the hem flared just enough to suggest movement without obscuring the body beneath.

Nell’s breath stuttered. “Where did you get those designs?”

Vivienne smiled. “From someone who had no right to them.”

A stagehand bumped into Nell from behind, muttering an apology. Nell didn’t acknowledge him. Her focus was on the dress. On the way the fabric draped over the hips, the way the sleeves hugged the arms like a second skin.

“You stole them,” Nell said.

“No,” Vivienne corrected. “I *reclaimed* them.”

Adrian Voss strode into the room, his presence immediately shrinking the space. He wore a black turtleneck, his signature look—simple, severe, designed to command attention without effort. His gaze locked onto Vivienne, then flicked to the dress. His jaw twitched.

Vivienne held his stare. “You always did hate sharing the spotlight.”

Adrian’s voice was low, controlled. “What are you doing here?”

“Supporting a friend’s show.” She gestured to the racks of clothing behind her—amples, toile samples, fabric swatches with her original sketches pinned to them. “Though I suppose after tonight, it won’t be *your* show anymore.”

Nell’s fingers tightened around the tablet. “Adrian, we need to talk about the collection. The buyers—”

“Are about to see *my* collection,” Adrian snapped. “The one I designed. The one I’ve been working on for *years*.”

Vivienne tilted her head. “Oh, Adrian. That’s where you’re wrong.”

A monitor mounted on the wall flickered to life. Behind them, the runway stretched into darkness, the lights still dimmed for the final pre-show checks. The screens above the catwalk glowed with a looping montage—flashes of Adrian’s signature pieces, intercut with close-ups of fabric textures, stitching patterns, color palettes.

Nell’s tablet chimed. Another news alert. She glanced down, then paled.

Adrian didn’t notice. He was too busy watching Vivienne, his eyes narrowing. “What did you do?”

Vivienne stepped closer. “I gave the press what they wanted. Proof.”

The monitor changed. The montage dissolved into a single image—a high-resolution scan of a Moreau Atelier client contract. Vivienne Moreau’s signature, unmistakable, dated years before Adrian’s first collection.

Nell’s gasp was audible. “You never signed those away.”

Adrian’s face twisted. “No. You—”

“No,” Vivienne interrupted. “You *assumed*. You took what you wanted, rewrote the history, and called it progress.” She gestured to the screens. “Every piece you’re about to show is built on my patterns, my clients, my reputation. And now the world knows it.”

A ripple of movement near the door. Julian Cross stood there, his coat damp from the sleet outside, his hands shoved into his pockets. His gaze flicked between Vivienne and Adrian, his expression unreadable.

Vivienne didn’t acknowledge him. Not yet.

Adrian’s voice dropped to a growl. “This is a disaster.”

Nell finally looked up from her tablet. Her voice was hollow. “The buyers are pulling out. Bergdorf’s already canceled their orders. The CFDA board is reviewing the nomination.” She swallowed. “Eleanor’s affair is everywhere. The press is calling it a corporate meltdown.”

Adrian’s hands clenched into fists. “You orchestrated this.”

Vivienne didn’t deny it. She simply adjusted the neckline of her gown, ensuring the seam lay perfectly against her skin. “I outmaneuvered you. Again.”

Julian took a step forward. “Vivi—”

She turned, her gaze sharp. “Not now, Julian.”

He stopped. The use of her childhood nickname hung between them, raw and intimate. Vivienne had never let anyone call her that in public.

Adrian followed Julian’s line of sight. His eyes flicked between them, something dark and ugly twisting in his expression. “You brought him into this?”

Vivienne ignored him. She reached into her clutch and pulled out a single object—a silver compact, polished to a mirror sheen. She flipped it open. Inside, a photograph curled at the edges. A younger Vivienne, her arm around a teenage Julian in her atelier, both of them grinning over a half-finished gown.

She snapped the compact shut and held it out to Julian. “You kept this.”

Julian didn’t take it. “I didn’t know what it meant until now.”

Adrian’s voice cut through the tension. “Enough.” He grabbed Vivienne’s wrist, his fingers digging into her skin. “This isn’t over.”

The movement was sudden, violent. The compact slipped from her grasp and hit the floor. The lid popped open. The photograph fluttered free.

Julian bent to retrieve it. His breath caught when he saw it.

Vivienne’s voice was calm, almost gentle. “Let go of me, Adrian. Or I’ll make sure every buyer, every investor, every critic in this room knows exactly how you *really* got your start.”

The music for Adrian’s show swelled through the speakers—a pounding, driving beat designed to rattle bones. The lights in the backstage area dimmed further, the glow of the runway seeping into the room like fog.

Adrian released her.

Vivienne smoothed her gown, then turned toward the monitors. The screens flickered again, the montage resetting—this time with a new image at the end. A single word, scrolling across the darkness in bold, white letters:

**PHANTOM**

Nell’s tablet chimed again. Another headline blazed across the screen:

*Voss & Co’s Designs Revealed as Moreau Atelier Revival—Runway Show Accused of Plagiarism*

Nell’s hands trembled. She looked at Vivienne, then at Adrian, then at Julian. The weight of what she’d enabled settled over her like a shroud.

Adrian grabbed the edge of a nearby table, his knuckles white. “You can’t do this. You don’t have the infrastructure. You don’t have the buyers. You don’t have the—”

“Yes,” Vivienne said. “I do.”

The house lights in the auditorium dimmed completely. The runway lights flared to life. The first model stepped onto the catwalk, Adrian’s signature silhouette cutting through the darkness.

But the screens behind her didn’t show Adrian’s collection.

They showed Vivienne’s.

Every stitch, every seam—familiar to anyone who had ever worked with Moreau Atelier. The audience murmured, some confused, some delighted, some already reaching for their phones to post the revelation online.

Adrian’s breath came fast. “Stop it.”

Vivienne smiled. “Too late.”

The music swelled. The crowd’s murmur turned to gasps, then applause. The cameras flashed. Phones lit up the room.

Nell’s phone buzzed again. This time, the screen displayed a single word:

**Help**

She didn’t answer it. Didn’t move.

Julian’s eyes locked onto Vivienne. “We need to talk. Now.”

Vivienne met his gaze. Something flickered between them—regret, maybe. Or understanding.

The auditorium erupted in applause.

Adrian’s voice was a blade. “You ruined me.”

Vivienne adjusted her gloves. “No, Adrian. I *remembered* you.”

Chapter 13

The Mercer Street showroom smelled of wool and cedar, the scent of men’s tailored suits and old money. Vivienne adjusted the lapels of her coat, the cashmere soft beneath her fingertips. The space was too warm, the air too thick with the weight of Adrian’s ambition.

Nell stood at the far end of the room, her back to the floor-to-ceiling windows. She hadn’t moved since the show ended, hadn’t spoken since the screens revealed Vivienne’s designs. Her tablet lay on the credenza beside her, the screen glowing with unread notifications.

Vivienne walked toward her, her heels silent on the hardwood.

Nell didn’t turn. “You could’ve warned me.”

“Would you have listened?”

A beat. Then Nell exhaled through her nose. “No.”

Vivienne stopped a few feet away. “I didn’t think so.”

Nell finally looked over her shoulder. Her mascara had smudged, just a little, from where she’d rubbed her eyes. “You let him take everything. Then you took it back.”

“Not everything,” Vivienne said. “Just what was mine.”

Nell turned fully, her hands flexing at her sides. “You made the client list public. Bergdorf’s called me personally. They’re canceling everything.”

Vivienne tilted her head. “Good.”

“And the press—”

“Is already calling Adrian a fraud.” Vivienne stepped closer. “You wrote the article, didn’t you?”

Nell’s throat worked. “What?”

“The *R fashion* piece. The one about Eleanor’s affair. About the corporate meltdown.” Vivienne kept her voice low, even. “You fed them the details. You wanted this to happen.”

Nell’s fingers curled into fists. “I wanted to understand.”

“Understand what?”

“*Why*.” Nell’s voice cracked. “Why you let him take your name. Why you let him rewrite your work. Why you let us all think you were gone.”

Vivienne didn’t flinch. “I didn’t let him do anything. I let him *believe* he could.”

Nell’s gaze dropped to Vivienne’s hands. To the gloves, the silver rings on her fingers. To the faint scar on her wrist, a mark from a long-ago fitting.

Then Nell saw it.

A stitch. A seam. A familiar curve of fabric—one Vivienne had sketched in her own hand, years ago, on a napkin in a café. The same silhouette that had just walked down Adrian’s runway.

Nell’s breath hitched.

Vivienne knew. She didn’t need to say it.

Nell reached out, her fingers trembling as she brushed the fabric at Vivienne’s wrist. The same pattern. The same hand. The same *everything*.

“No,” Nell whispered.

Vivienne didn’t pull away. “Yes.”

Nell’s hand fell. “I used those sketches. I finished them. I—” She cut herself off, her face twisting. “God. I submitted them as *his* work.”

Vivienne’s silence was answer enough.

Nell staggered back, her hip hitting the credenza. The tablet tilted, its screen flickering. “I taught you those techniques. The French seams. The understitched edges. The way to—” She swallowed. “The way to make the fabric *breathe*.”

Vivienne adjusted her coat, smoothing the front. “And now you recognize them.”

Nell’s voice dropped to a rasp. “You let me.”

Vivienne met her stare. “You didn’t ask.”

Nell’s phone buzzed in her pocket. She fumbled for it, her hands shaking. The screen lit up—Eleanor’s name flashed in bold letters.

Nell’s thumb hovered over the answer button.

Vivienne watched, her pulse steady.

Nell’s finger twitched. Then she silenced the call. The phone went dark in her palm.

Vivienne exhaled. “You could’ve walked away.”

“So could you.”

“And left him to take everything?” Vivienne shook her head. “No.”

Nell’s jaw tightened. “You’re not just reclaiming your designs. You’re ruining him.”

Vivienne stepped closer. “I’m reclaiming what’s mine. The rest is consequence.”

Nell’s phone buzzed again. The same name. The same screen.

Vivienne’s gaze flicked to it. “You should answer.”

Nell didn’t move. “Why?”

“Because she’s your sister.” Vivienne’s voice was quiet. “And she’s begging for help.”

Nell’s breath came fast. “After everything.”

Vivienne didn’t argue. She simply waited.

Nell’s hands trembled as she lifted the phone. She stared at the screen, her thumb hovering.

Then she thumbed the decline button.

The call ended.

Nell’s exhale was shaky. “Done.”

Vivienne nodded. “Good.”

Nell’s phone buzzed a third time. She stared at the screen, then flicked the device onto the credenza. It landed with a dull *clack*.

Vivienne reached into her coat pocket and pulled out a small envelope. She held it out.

Nell didn’t take it. “What is it?”

“Your severance.” Vivienne’s voice was even. “Ten million. A letter of recommendation. And a non-disclosure agreement.”

Nell’s eyes burned. “You’re firing me.”

“I’m freeing you.” Vivienne dropped the envelope onto the credenza beside Nell’s phone. “Take it. Or leave. But don’t stay out of guilt.”

Nell’s fingers curled around the envelope’s edge. She didn’t open it.

Vivienne turned toward the door. “The offer expires at midnight.”

Nell’s voice stopped her. “Wait.”

Vivienne paused.

Nell’s throat worked. “Why did you let me see the dress?”

Vivienne didn’t turn. “Because you needed to remember.”

Nell’s voice was raw. “Remember what?”

Vivienne pulled the door open. “That you were *mine* first.”

The door clicked shut behind her.

Nell stood alone in the showroom, the weight of the envelope in her hand, the weight of her choices pressing down.

Her phone buzzed again.

She didn’t look at it.

Outside, the sleet had turned to rain, the droplets sliding down the windows like tears.

Chapter 14

The penthouse suite at the Four Seasons smelled of bergamot and old leather. Adrian stood at the floor-to-ceiling windows, a glass of bourbon in his hand, the city lights blurring beyond the glass. The CFDA nomination plaque on the mantel reflected the flicker of the television—some analyst praising his "bold vision" while trashing Moreau Atelier as a "relic."

The door clicked open.

Adrian didn’t turn. “You’re early.”

Vivienne stepped inside, her coat damp at the shoulders from the February drizzle. She removed her gloves one finger at a time, her rings catching the light. “No. You’re late.”

He swirled the amber liquid in his glass. “I was busy.”

“So was I.” She hung her coat on the rack, the fabric whispering against the wood. “But you know how it is—power waits for no one.”

Adrian finally faced her. His smile was sharp, practiced. “You lost that privilege when you signed the dissolution papers.”

“Did I?” Vivienne crossed the room, her heels muted on the plush rug. “Funny. I don’t remember signing anything permanent.”

He laughed, a short bark. “You were forced out. The board voted. The records are sealed.”

“Sealed by *you*.” She stopped at the wet bar, poured herself two fingers of the same bourbon. “Funny how that works.”

Adrian’s grip tightened on his glass. “You don’t have anything left, Vivienne. The ateliers are shuttered. The clients are gone. Your name’s a ghost.”

“Is it?” She took a sip, the burn of the alcohol nothing compared to the cold in her veins. “Because I seem to recall a certain CFDA nomination being built on *my* designs. *My* patterns. *My* clients.”

His jaw twitched. “Those were *my* shows.”

“Were they?” Vivienne set her glass down. “Tell me, Adrian—when did you last cut a toile? When did you last sit at a sewing machine until your fingers bled? When did you *design* anything that wasn’t already sketched in my hand?”

Adrian’s mouth opened. Closed.

Vivienne stepped closer. “You took my name. My legacy. My *life’s work*. And you did it with Nell’s help, didn’t you? My former protégé. My *friend*.”

“She betrayed you,” Adrian said, but the words lacked conviction.

“Did she?” Vivienne tilted her head. “Or did she just follow the man who promised her power?”

A muscle jumped in Adrian’s cheek. “What do you want?”

Vivienne pulled an envelope from her coat. Thick, official. “Your pen.”

He didn’t move. “For what?”

She held up the envelope. “Fifty-one percent of Voss & Co. Signed over to me. Today.”

Adrian’s laugh was hollow. “You’re joking.”

“Am I?” Vivienne tossed the envelope onto the coffee table. It landed with a soft *thud*. “Open it.”

He didn’t. “In exchange for what?”

“Nothing.” She gestured to the papers inside. “Your shares. Your name on the masthead. Your precious CFDA campaign—*as long as it’s built on your own talent for once*.”

Adrian’s fingers twitched toward the envelope, then recoiled. “This is blackmail.”

“No.” Vivienne smiled. “This is *justice*.” She picked up a pen from the desk, clicked it open. “Sign. Or I release the forensic audit.”

He went still. “What audit?”

“Full disclosure.” She tapped the pen against her palm. “Every stitch, every seam, every stolen design from the last decade. The *R fashion* exposé. The Bergdorf’s cancellation. The SEC filing.” Her voice dropped. “The *murder* of Moreau Atelier.”

Adrian’s face drained of color. “You wouldn’t.”

“I already did.” Vivienne set the pen down. “Nell just hasn’t told you yet. Out of loyalty. Or pity.”

Adrian’s hand shook as he picked up the envelope. His thumb traced the embossed logo at the corner—Voss & Co. The irony wasn’t lost on either of them.

Vivienne watched him, her pulse steady. “You have until I count to ten.”

He flipped open the envelope. Scanned the first page. His breath hitched.

“One.”

Adrian’s pen hovered over the signature line.

“Two.”

He licked his lips.

“Three.”

The pen pressed into the paper, ink blooming like a bruise.

“Four.”

Adrian signed.

Vivienne picked up the papers, folded them with deliberate care. “Good boy.”

He flinched like she’d struck him.

Vivienne set the signed documents on the table. “Now. There’s one more thing.”

Adrian’s glass hit the bar with a crack. “What?”

She walked to the door, opened it. “Nell.”

Nell stood in the hallway, her coat damp, her face pale. She hadn’t taken her eyes off Vivienne since she entered.

Vivienne stepped aside. “You can stay.”

Nell swallowed. “As what?”

“My COO.” Vivienne’s voice left no room for negotiation. “But not as my enemy.”

Adrian’s laugh was bitter. “She betrayed you. More than once.”

Nell didn’t look at him. “And you let him rewrite your work. Every day.”

Vivienne held out her hand. “Choose.”

Nell’s fingers trembled as she took it.

Adrian’s voice cut through the room like a blade. “This isn’t over.”

Vivienne turned. Her gaze was ice. “It is for you.”

Silence settled, thick and suffocating.

Then the door to the hallway opened wider.

Julian stood there, his sleeves rolled to the elbows, his hands stained with ink. He held a sketchbook. Old. Leather-bound. The spine cracked from use.

His eyes locked onto Vivienne’s.

She didn’t move.

Julian stepped forward, his voice rough. “Is this hers?”

Vivienne’s throat tightened. “Where did you find it?”

“In the trash.” His fingers tightened around the book. “Adrian’s trash. After the show. I pulled it out before the janitors took it.”

Nell’s breath hitched.

Vivienne’s hands stayed at her sides. “What’s in it?”

Julian opened the book. Flipped to a page. A sketch—faded, but unmistakable. A coat. A collar. The stitching Vivienne had taught him herself, years ago, in a tiny ateliers on Rue Cambon.

His voice dropped. “You were—”

Vivienne’s pulse roared in her ears.

Julian’s gaze flicked to her. To the rings on her fingers. To the scar on her wrist.

“You,” he said. “You were my teacher.”

Vivienne didn’t speak.

Adrian’s voice was a snarl. “You *knew*?”

Julian didn’t look at him. “I didn’t *know*. Not until now.”

Vivienne’s chest ached. She wanted to reach for him. To pull him close. To feel the weight of him against her.

But the room was too small. The air too charged.

Julian stepped closer. “Can I work for you?”

Vivienne’s voice was barely a whisper. “Do you want to?”

He held up the sketchbook. “I want to finish what you started.”

Vivienne’s throat burned. She nodded.

Julian exhaled, long and slow.

Adrian’s glass shattered on the floor.

No one looked.

Vivienne turned to Nell. “Get the jet ready. We leave at dawn.”

Nell’s voice was steady. “Where?”

Vivienne adjusted her coat one last time. “Home.”

She stepped into the hallway.

Julian followed.

The door clicked shut behind them.

Downstairs, the elevators dinged. The city hummed, indifferent, beneath them.

Somewhere, a champagne cork popped.

The game had changed. Again.

Chapter 15

The Mercer Street showroom gleamed under the late afternoon sun. The iron gates had been pried open, the Voss & Co sign still warm in Vivienne’s hands. She turned it over, letting the afternoon light catch the fleur-de-lis etched into the metal.

Nell adjusted the Moreau Atelier crest above the door, her fingers trembling just enough to betray her. “It’s too big,” she muttered.

“No.” Vivienne set the sign down. “It’s exactly right. Like a birthmark.”

Julian stood in the center of the room, surrounded by half-unpacked crates of Moreau fabrics. He held a bolt of silk the color of wet slate, running it through his fingers. “This is the same stuff you used for the *Nocturne* line.”

Vivienne didn’t answer. She walked to the drafting table, picked up a half-finished sketch—a dress with a bodice that flared like a peacock’s tail. She held it out to Nell.

Nell didn’t take it. “You’re starting tomorrow.”

“Of course.” Vivienne set the sketch back down. “But a queen doesn’t sit on a new throne without knowing its curves.”

Julian’s voice was quiet. “You’re not just reclaiming the brand. You’re redesigning it.”

Vivienne looked at him. Really looked. He was thinner than she remembered, the hollows beneath his cheekbones more pronounced. But his hands—those hands still knew how to hold a needle, how to shape a sleeve, how to make fabric breathe.

“Yes,” she said. “And you’ll help me.”

Nell finally took the sketch. Held it to the light. “You’re not doing the classic Moreau revival.”

“No.” Vivienne walked to the window, looked out at the street. A couple of photographers lingered outside, phones poised. Word had spread faster than ink on silk. “The world thinks Moreau Atelier is dead. I want them to wonder if they ever really knew it at all.”

Julian set the bolt of silk down. “What does that mean?”

Vivienne turned. Smiled. “It means the *Phantom* collection wasn’t my last. It’s my first.”

Nell dropped the sketch. “Jesus, Vivienne.”

“No.” Vivienne’s voice was steel. “It’s *Moreau*.”

A flashbulb popped outside. Vivienne didn’t flinch.

Julian picked up a pair of scissors from the table, tested the blades between his fingers. “You’re going public. With a show. In two weeks.”

“Yes.”

“You’ll have to face the press. The critics. The board.”

“Yes.”

“You’ll have to face *him*.”

Vivienne’s smile didn’t waver. “I already did. And he signed the papers.”

Nell exhaled, sharp and sudden. “You played this like a chess game. Not a knife fight.”

“No.” Vivienne picked up a swatch of emerald green, the same shade as the first dress she’d ever sold. “It was always ballet. Graceful. Precise. Until it wasn’t.”

Julian put the scissors down. “You’re not afraid.”

“I’m terrified.” Vivienne folded the swatch into her pocket. “But fear is for people who have something left to lose.”

Nell looked at Julian. Then back at Vivienne. “What do we tell the investors?”

Vivienne walked to the drafting table, picked up a pen. She drew a single line—a swoop, a curve, the silhouette of a coat. “That Moreau Atelier is back. And this time, we answer to no one.”

Julian stepped closer. “You’re cutting them loose.”

“They cut themselves loose when they chose him over me.” Vivienne capped the pen. “Now they’ll choose again.”

Nell’s phone buzzed. She checked the screen. Her face went pale. “It’s Clara Reyes.”

Vivienne didn’t move. “Answer it.”

Nell swiped. Put the phone to her ear. “Hello?”

Silence. Then Nell’s voice, tight. “Yes, Clara, I—”

A pause. Nell’s fingers whitened around the phone. “No. That’s not possible.”

Vivienne held out her hand.

Nell didn’t give her the phone. But she turned the speaker on.

A woman’s voice, smooth and amused. “Tell your boss the Bergdorf Goodman audit is live. Every stitch in every collection? Every bolt of fabric? Every client receipt? All traceable to Moreau Atelier. Even the ones with another label.”

Vivienne didn’t react. But Julian did. His breath caught.

Clara’s voice continued. “They’re calling it the greatest intellectual property heist in fashion history. And Adrian Voss is about to be the poster child for corporate kleptocracy.”

Nell’s voice was a rasp. “Clara, wait—”

“Too late for warnings, Nell.” Clara’s laugh was soft, almost gentle. “The press is already at his door. Your brother’s too, I hear. Funny how these things circle back.”

The line went dead.

Nell dropped the phone. It clattered against the concrete floor.

Julian’s voice was raw. “What did she mean about your brother?”

Nell’s eyes filled. “He’s not my brother. Not by blood. He’s—and Adrian paid to suppress the birth records. To keep me silent.” She swallowed. “He’s your son, Julian.”

Vivienne didn’t move. Didn’t breathe. Didn’t speak.

Julian’s face twisted. “That’s—that’s impossible.”

Nell’s tears spilled over. “I was sixteen. He was thirty-two. He told me it was love. That we’d be a family. That he’d protect me.” She laughed, wet and broken. “And I believed him.”

Julian’s legs gave out. He sat heavily on the floor, his back against the drafting table. His hands covered his face.

Vivienne knelt in front of him. Took his wrists. Pulled his hands away.

His eyes were wild. “You knew.”

“No.” Vivienne shook her head. “But I suspected. The age gap. The way he looked at you. The way you—” She stopped. Swallowed. “The way you protected him. Like a mother protecting her child.”

Julian’s voice was a whisper. “He used me.”

“And you used him back.” Vivienne stood. “You gave me the sketches. You held the pattern cutter’s blade against his throat. You finished what I started.”

Nell sobbed. “I didn’t know. Not until Clara called today. She’s been digging for years. She found the hospital records. The adoption files. The payments to the midwife who—”

Julian stood abruptly. “I need air.”

He walked to the door, pushed it open. The sunlight hit him full in the face.

Vivienne followed. Stopped just outside the threshold.

Julian didn’t turn around. “Does he know?”

“I don’t know.” Vivienne’s voice was quiet. “But if he does, he’s known for years.”

Julian’s shoulders shook. Not from cold. From the weight of something he’d never carried before.

Vivienne stepped closer. Not touching. Just close enough to feel the heat of him.

Julian finally turned. His eyes were red. “What do I do?”

Vivienne looked at him. Really looked.

She said nothing.

Julian’s breath hitched. His gaze dropped to her mouth.

Vivienne didn’t move. Didn’t speak. Didn’t break the silence.

Julian leaned in.

The door behind them creaked open. Nell stood there, her face streaked with tears, her phone clutched in her hand.

She didn’t say a word.

She didn’t have to.

The moment shattered.

Julian stepped back. His face was a mask of shame and longing and something like fury.

Vivienne turned away. Walked back into the showroom. Her heels clicked against the concrete.

Nell didn’t follow.

Vivienne picked up the half-finished sketch. The dart of the sleeve. The curve of the hem.

She looked at the swatch in her pocket.

The emerald green. The color of a first love. The color of a child’s first dress.

She slipped her hand into her pocket. Pulled out the swatch.

Held it out.

To Nell.

“You,” Vivienne said. “You designed this.”

Nell’s breath hitched. She took the swatch. Ran it between her fingers.

Vivienne smiled. Just a little.

“Welcome home.”
